item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good idea +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing previous comments on the ratio of extraneous material vs. the stuff that works is somewhat tenuous, but it ultimately worked for me. Barely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an amazing article that's only brought down by its ending. Everything before the O5 email collapsible was super evocative (as people have said up-thread) and had scary implications, but the actual ending (along with the hidden collapsible) feel like they're forced into the story to give it some kind of grander end, when really the last audio log with Marsh was enough. The ending isn't enough to impact my upvote, but I do wish it ended with Marsh's last audio log (not including the hidden one).

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"due to accessing non-selfed memories stored within SCP-3100"

maybe this is a stupid, pedantic nitpick, but I feel like "non-self" or "un-selfed" would flow more smoothly here.

+1 tho, even if I'm a touch confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought it was very interesting and luckily clicked at the end to what may have been intended by the author.
I think there is a link to a rather important SCP in there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I really like this, and hiding the last addendum was very clever (I only discovered it while source-mining an older version), I feel hiding the last addendum/message might actually be detrimental. Most people will come and go without ever seeing the bit most crucial to understanding the end. Anyway, it was well-executed and the premise vaguely reminds me of the Enigma of Amigara Fault. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellently crafted, providing just enough pieces of the puzzle to get a bit of a picture, but then letting your mind take over and scare you with the possibilities of the rest. I love the feel of such a primal, subterranean, horror (and especially the added depth included by the basic human fear of what could possibly lie after death). This was one of the most evocative entries I've read in awhile. After reading this and SCP-3000, I have very high hopes for the rest of series 4!
In short, +1 :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice work on this

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Updated for it not to be a cat anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you sure it's a coincidence? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, that seems to be a mistake. Fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just on the edge of being not enough to interest me. Much of it is well written but doesn't interest me; the description of the mummies was super evocative, though, and I liked the ancient horror stuff. I kind of wish there was more meat and less fluff, but upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, "Anantashesha", and "To Force the Hand of God", strike me as similar, in being a long piece in which several exploration logs uncover some ancient horror that the Foundation has discovered, of those three I feel this is the best one. The core concept here is both creepy in the cave full of mummies way, and horrific in a few well-worn but masterfully executed ways.

One question, why are the older bodies deeper? In most catacombs of this kind the opposite is true, people put the bodies near the entrance at first and as time goes on are forced deeper to find unused space.

That thing in the last image still looks like a cat, and I can't see it as anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realized, this document is seen from different perspectives like 2317, since we're a new operative to Task Force 707 in one section and an O5 in another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what I did for a stint, before here? Out-processing. Faced men and women even older than I was, told them to either pick the needle or the bullet.

Aaaaand upvoted.

All-together I quite like this, and as the logs themselves pointed out, it's a nice chance from the usual memory-obliterating trope. Also tweaks my fetishes for ancient civilizations, philosophical commentary, underground labyrinths and the foundation getting called out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As near as I can determine, the geographical coordinates are somewhere in the Indian Ocean. Is this significant to the SCP in some way?

Also, great backgrounding and characterization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could the author provide spoilers about what's going on? It's interesting, but I can't really figure it out.

+ show comments
- hide comments

Seems to me that the most obvious use of such an SCP would be for posthumous "interrogation" of moles, enemies and Persons of Interest. They might decide against it based on the fear that the dead could have memories of memetic hazards and/or infohazards the dead were inoculated against, but I think that this idea being considered and dismissed would at least be mentioned.

So, the tunnel. The tunnel went straight on down for another, what, forty? Fifty metres? I couldn't tell from sight alone, but the torchbeam seemed to indicate as much. And over on the left and right, more of those little shelves just going all the way down into the dark.

1) If it's vertical it should probably be called a shaft.

2) If niches are present even on the sides of vertical shafts, that should probably be noted at the beginning of the description.

Recovered entries dated 2011/10-2012/01 from the work journal of Dr. N. Coetzee.

Since this happened months before everything went FUBAR, shouldn't those notes have been handed over to the Foundation for archiving, and hence not be "recovered"?

Senior Agents G. Chandrasekar, M. Radler and Ishikawa K. are found with unopened autoinjector sachets of Class-A amnestic in their hands. Senior Operative G. Chandrasekar demonstrates symptoms of neurochemical shock, likely due to negative interactions between the Class-A amnestic

If their autoinjectors were unopened, doesn't that mean that three other team members used their autoinjectors on them, meaning there would be three members who hadn't amnesticized themselves?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would be fucking meta as hell…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat.

Initially, as I was reading this, I was reminded of the Seance Dust from All Hallows, but then the story went further and further down the rabbit hole, and became creepier and creepier.

While this doesn't quite bring me to the point of outright fear, its wonderfully tense and atmospheric and a solid entry.

+1 minmin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honest to god, between that line through the photo and the location of those coordinates (plus the stuff about the void after death), I thought this was the Anantashesha entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, too, was confused by the last two pictures (it's definitely a big cat!), but this is otherwise fantastic. I'm surprised it's not Thaumiel, given the high infosec value. Also, there are a couple extra \'s in there, though given they're in the "DATA CORRUPT" sections, that might be okay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the hominid on its hands and knees? Because if it is, then I can definitely see that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's pretty much where I'm at. I just don't know how to integrate the events of the last couple of logs, let alone end up with the email from the O5 and what's shown in the picture.

I like Maas's exposition and a lot of that content and themes before that, but I'm just not sure what's going on.

Also, the date in the last picture is way off from the dated events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really it looks like a cat to me… Weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I'm missing the one key thing that will tie this all together in my mind

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aye, I see. At least I was right in guessing that it was some sort of entity with feet and a body, but I probably only got that from context clues right before it. Obscurity in the image doesn't detract from the article's weight at all, though, so you're good on that. This was probably one of my favorite entries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's meant to be some kind of hominid but I can understand if it's way out of left field.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Looks like a silhouette of a gigantic cat to me. One that is less IR reflective than the background.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is deeply disturbing, but I'm not sure what my eyes are supposed to be making out in the final image. (Unless not having any idea of what it is is the point.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ crit stuff
- neerrrrrrrrd

have been denied on safety grounds.

"on safety grounds" sounds off - maybe "on ground of safety concerns" or something else?

Even if what we find down here, doesn't make it to the surface.

That comma is weirdly placed.

The Foundation needs its dead, and the dead needs your tongues.

My English is butts sometimes, but shouldn't "needs" be "need?"

wrapped around and touched something you aren't being supposed to.

Is this supposed to read "touched by?"

Other bits:
The corrupted data sections would be a little more visually interesting if the featured more visible corruption within the document.

- neerrrrrrrrd

Overall: 8/10, was well executed enough to upvote but not quuuuiiiite evocative enough for me to love it. Still very competent and the bits I pointed out as weird were all minor, regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image credits:

1) CC 4.0 image by Wikimedia user Mike Peel. Location is the Sterkfontein caves in Gauteng province, South Africa. Sourced from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sterkfontein_Caves_41.jpg

2) CC 4.0 image by Wikimedia user Kwangmo, from what I assume is the Seoul Metropolitan Fire & Disaster Headquarters. Depicts a victim of the 2008 Sichuan Earthquake. Sourced from here.

3) CC 3.0 image by Wikimedia user Sailko, of the Grotarrosa mummy. Sourced from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mummia_di_grottarossa,_bambina_di_circa_8_anni,_da_via_cassia_loc._la_giustiniana,_150-200_dc_ca..JPG

3) Public domain image of the Vredefort crater by NASA. Digital colouration is mine. Sourced from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Vredefort_crater.jpg

4) Public domain image: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Anochecer_en_Galicia.JPG . CC 4.0 image uploaded by user Jordiferror: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:2016_CosmoCaixa_014.jpg

Review credits: Reject, vezaz, Taffeta, Silber, Scantron, Rimple, ChaoSera, and probably a couple I forget. I missed the 1k and 2k contests despite being technically around for either, so this really means a lot to me. I hope this does well.

Also, if the image(s) aren't okay, they can be removed.
